---
title: "Databases & SQL: Querying Where the Data Lives"
unit: "II"
lesson: "02"
type: concept
tags: [databases, sql, duckdb, olap, oltp]
difficulty: introductory
duration: "50 mins"
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

- **Explain** what a relational table is and read a basic `SELECT` statement. *(Understand)*
- **Write** a `SELECT … WHERE … GROUP BY … ORDER BY` query to answer a question. *(Apply)*
- **Decide** when a columnar/OLAP engine like DuckDB beats loading everything into pandas. *(Analyze)*

> **Where this sits:** after **L01 — What Is Data Science?** · before **L03 — Storage
> Formats: CSV vs Parquet** (first lesson of Unit II).

> **Hands-on:** [Lab — Querying Files with DuckDB](l02_lab_querying_duckdb.md) ·
> [![Open in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/{{< var repo.org >}}/{{< var repo.name >}}/blob/{{< var repo.branch >}}/u02_big_data/l02_lab_querying_duckdb.ipynb)

## Why This Matters

In L01 we framed a question and said *"acquire the data."* Most useful data already lives
in a **database** — and you don't move it to analyze it; you **ask it questions** in SQL,
the language that has run the data world for 50 years.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
A database is a well-organized warehouse and SQL is the order form. You don't wander the
aisles (loop over rows) — you hand in a form describing *what* you want, and the warehouse
staff (the query engine) figure out the fastest way to fetch it.
:::

## Intuition: Tables and SELECT

A **relational table** is rows (records) × columns (fields) with names and types — a
spreadsheet with rules. You retrieve from it with `SELECT`:

```sql
SELECT department, AVG(gpa) AS avg_gpa   -- which columns (and computed values)
FROM students                            -- which table
WHERE enrolled = TRUE                    -- keep only some rows
GROUP BY department                      -- one output row per department
ORDER BY avg_gpa DESC;                   -- sort the result
```

Read it in **execution order**, not written order: `FROM -> WHERE -> GROUP BY -> SELECT ->
ORDER BY`. You describe the *result you want*; the engine decides *how* to compute it.
That declarative style is why the same query runs on a laptop or a cluster unchanged.

## OLTP vs OLAP — Two Jobs, Two Layouts

| | **OLTP** (transactional) | **OLAP** (analytical) |
|---|---|---|
| Job | run the business: insert/update one row at a time | understand the business: scan millions of rows, few columns |
| Example | "record this enrollment" | "average GPA by department" |
| Engine | PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite | **DuckDB**, BigQuery, Snowflake |
| Storage | **row**-oriented | **column**-oriented |

Why the storage layout matters for *our* work (analysis = OLAP):

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
    subgraph Row["Row store"]
      r1["(1, Ana, CS, 3.6)"] --- r2["(2, Leo, Math, 2.9)"]
    end
    subgraph Col["Column store"]
      c1["gpa: 3.6, 2.9, 3.1, …"] --- c2["dept: CS, Math, CS, …"]
    end
    Row ~~~ Col
```

*Top: a row store keeps each record's fields together. Bottom: a column store keeps each
column's values together.*

To average one column over a million rows, a **column store reads just that column** off
disk (and compresses it well, because neighbors are similar). A row store must touch every
row. That is the whole reason analytical queries are dramatically faster on columnar
engines — you'll *measure* this later in Unit II.

## Why DuckDB

**DuckDB is an in-process OLAP database** — "SQLite for analytics." No server to install
(`pip install duckdb`), and it queries data files and pandas DataFrames **directly**,
including files bigger than RAM. It's the ideal teaching and analysis engine: real SQL,
columnar speed, zero setup.

## When to Use SQL/DuckDB — and When NOT To

- **Use it when:** the data is in a database or a data file; you're
  aggregating/filtering/joining; the data is large; you want a query that's readable and
  portable.
- **Reach for pandas instead when:** you need row-wise Python logic, plotting, feature
  engineering, or reshaping that's awkward in SQL. (They compose — DuckDB can query a
  pandas DataFrame and hand the result back as one.)

## Quiz Hooks

*Feeds the retrieval quiz at the start of the next session.*

- The clause order `FROM -> WHERE -> GROUP BY -> SELECT -> ORDER BY` <!-- obj 1 · Understand · Moodle MC -->
- **OLTP vs OLAP** and which storage layout each uses <!-- obj 3 · Analyze · Moodle MC -->
- Writing a `GROUP BY` aggregation <!-- obj 2 · Apply · CodeRunner -->

## FAQ / Industry Reality

**"If I know pandas, why learn SQL?"** — Because the data usually arrives *in* a database,
and pushing the filter/aggregation to the engine (instead of loading everything into
Python) is faster, uses less memory, and is what every data team actually does. SQL is
also far more portable across jobs and tools than any one library's API.

**"Is DuckDB a 'real' database?"** — Yes, for analytics. It's not for high-concurrency
transactional writes (that's PostgreSQL's job) — it's for *reading and crunching*, which
is what analysis is.

## Where to Go Deeper

- **Official docs:** [DuckDB — SQL Introduction](https://duckdb.org/docs/stable/sql/introduction) and [Data Import](https://duckdb.org/docs/stable/data/overview) — excellent, example-driven.
- **Interactive reference:** [Mode SQL Tutorial — GROUP BY](https://mode.com/sql-tutorial/sql-group-by) and aggregate functions (free).
- **Concept article:** [Why DuckDB](https://duckdb.org/why_duckdb) — the case for in-process, columnar analytics.
